source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
title: Ensure that encryption is enabled for EFS file systems

input_row.source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure that encryption is enabled for EFS file systems
input_row.description: EFS data should be encrypted at rest using AWS KMS (Key Management Service).
input_row.rationale: Data should be encrypted at rest to reduce the risk of a data breach via direct access to the storage device.
input_row.audit: From Console: 1. Login to the AWS Management Console and Navigate to the Elastic File System (EFS) dashboard. 2. Select File Systems from the left navigation panel. 3. Each item on the list has a visible Encrypted field that displays data at rest encryption status. 4. Validate that this field reads Encrypted for all EFS file systems in all AWS regions. From CLI: 1. Run the describe-file-systems command using custom query filters to list the identifiers of all AWS EFS file systems currently available within the selected region: aws efs describe-file-systems --region <region> --output table --query 'FileSystems[*].FileSystemId' 2. The command output should return a table with the requested file system IDs. 3. Run the describe-file-systems command using the ID of the file system that you want to examine as file-system-id and the necessary query filters: aws efs describe-file-systems --region <region> --file-system-id <file- system-id> --query 'FileSystems[*].Encrypted' 4. The command output should return the file system encryption status as true or false. If the returned value is false, the selected AWS EFS file system is not encrypted and if the returned value is true, the selected AWS EFS file system is encrypted.
input_row.remediation: It is important to note that EFS file system data-at-rest encryption must be turned on when creating the file system. If an EFS file system has been created without data-at-rest encryption enabled, then you must create another EFS file system with the correct configuration and transfer the data. Steps to create an EFS file system with data encrypted at rest: From Console: 1. Login to the AWS Management Console and Navigate to the Elastic File System (EFS) dashboard. 2. Select File Systems from the left navigation panel. 3. Click the Create File System button from the dashboard top menu to start the file system setup process. 4. On the Configure file system access configuration page, perform the following actions: • Choose an appropriate VPC from the VPC dropdown list. • Within the Create mount targets section, check the boxes for all of the Availability Zones (AZs) within the selected VPC. These will be your mount targets. • Click Next step to continue. 5. Perform the following on the Configure optional settings page: • Create tags to describe your new file system. • Choose performance mode based on your requirements. • Check the Enable encryption box and choose aws/elasticfilesystem from the Select KMS master key dropdown list to enable encryption for the new file system, using the default master key provided and managed by AWS KMS. • Click Next step to continue. 6. Review the file system configuration details on the review and create page and then click Create File System to create your new AWS EFS file system. 7. Copy the data from the old unencrypted EFS file system onto the newly created encrypted file system. 8. Remove the unencrypted file system as soon as your data migration to the newly created encrypted file system is completed. 9. Change the AWS region from the navigation bar and repeat the entire process for the other AWS regions.  From CLI: 1. Run the describe-file-systems command to view the configuration information for the selected unencrypted file system identified in the Audit steps: aws efs describe-file-systems --region <region> --file-system-id <file- system-id> 2. The command output should return the configuration information. 3. To provision a new AWS EFS file system, you need to generate a universally unique identifier (UUID) to create the token required by the create-file- system command. To create the required token, you can use a randomly generated UUID from "https://www.uuidgenerator.net". 4. Run the create-file-system command using the unique token created at the previous step: aws efs create-file-system --region <region> --creation-token <uuid> -- performance-mode generalPurpose --encrypted 5. The command output should return the new file system configuration metadata. 6. Run the create-mount-target command using the EFS file system ID returned from step 4 as the identifier and the ID of the Availability Zone (AZ) that will represent the mount target: aws efs create-mount-target --region <region> --file-system-id <file-system- id> --subnet-id <subnet-id> 7. The command output should return the new mount target metadata. 8. Now you can mount your file system from an EC2 instance. 9. Copy the data from the old unencrypted EFS file system to the newly created encrypted file system. 10. Remove the unencrypted file system as soon as your data migration to the newly created encrypted file system is completed: aws efs delete-file-system --region <region> --file-system-id <unencrypted- file-system-id> 11. Change the AWS region by updating the --region and repeat the entire process for the other AWS regions. Default Value: EFS file system data is encrypted at rest by default when creating a file system through the Console. However, encryption at rest is not enabled by default when creating a new file system using the AWS CLI, API, or SDKs. References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/efs/latest/ug/encryption-at-rest.html 2. https://awscli.amazonaws.com/v2/documentation/api/latest/reference/efs/index.ht ml#efs

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

- ✓ Can AWS APIs query this property? → Yes, AWS APIs can query the encryption status of EFS file systems.
- ✓ Is this a standard AWS service property (encryption, logging, access control, network rules)? → Yes, encryption is a standard property for AWS resources like EFS.
- ✓ Does boto3 SDK have methods to check this? → Yes, boto3 can describe EFS file systems and check their encryption status.
- ✓ Does AWS CLI have commands to retrieve this? → Yes, the AWS CLI has commands to check EFS encryption status.
- ✓ Does audit mention boto3/AWS CLI commands? → Yes, the audit instructions specifically include AWS CLI commands.
- ✓ Is it about IAM, S3, EC2, VPC, CloudTrail, Config, KMS, etc.? → Yes, it involves an AWS service and encryption, similar to services like KMS.

Given these considerations, the control can be fully verified using AWS APIs, boto3, or AWS CLI, making it suitable for automation.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_efs_file_system_encryption_at_rest_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

- **AWS API Calls:** Use the `boto3` SDK to call `efs.describe_file_systems()` to check the encryption status of all EFS file systems across regions.
- **AWS CLI Commands:** Use `aws efs describe-file-systems --region <region> --query 'FileSystems[*].Encrypted'` to retrieve the encryption status.
- **Resources to Enumerate:** Enumerate all EFS file systems across all AWS regions for a given account.
- **Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:** Validate that each EFS file system has the `Encrypted` property set to `true`.
- **Pass/Fail Criteria:** 
  - **Pass:** All EFS file systems have `Encrypted: true`.
  - **Fail:** Any EFS file system has `Encrypted: false`.
- **Example Validation:** Ensure that for every file system returned by `efs.describe_file_systems()`, the `Encrypted` field is `true`.

This automated approach allows for checking the encryption status of EFS file systems quickly and efficiently across multiple regions, ensuring compliance with the CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The encryption status of EFS file systems can be checked using AWS APIs, boto3, and AWS CLI, which makes automation feasible and efficient. The use of these tools aligns with AWS SDK capabilities and security best practices.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9

- The automation details are technically accurate. The use of `boto3` with `efs.describe_file_systems()` and the AWS CLI command `aws efs describe-file-systems --region <region> --query 'FileSystems[*].Encrypted'` are valid methods for retrieving the encryption status of EFS file systems.
- The program name `aws_efs_file_system_encryption_at_rest_enabled` is descriptive and follows a clear naming convention, indicating the specific security check being performed.
- The assessment correctly identifies the pass/fail criteria based on the `Encrypted` property.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Consideration of AWS Config:** While the current approach is effective, incorporating AWS Config rules could enhance continuous compliance monitoring. AWS Config has a managed rule `efs-encrypted-check` that can automatically evaluate the encryption status of EFS file systems.
- **Cross-Region Enumeration:** Ensure that the automation script handles pagination and iterates over all AWS regions, as EFS file systems can exist in any region.
- **Error Handling:** Implement error handling in the automation script to manage API call failures or permission issues gracefully.
- **Program Name Suggestion:** The current name is clear, but for consistency with AWS naming conventions, consider `aws_efs_encryption_at_rest_check` to emphasize the encryption aspect.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded, leveraging AWS API capabilities and established security practices. The automation approach is technically sound and aligns with AWS's recommended methods for verifying encryption settings.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- **Keep as-is** with minor enhancements suggested in the improvements section. The current approach is robust and effectively utilizes AWS tools for compliance verification. Incorporating AWS Config and improving error handling would further strengthen the solution.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identify that the encryption status of AWS EFS file systems is directly accessible via AWS APIs (boto3, AWS CLI) and is a standard property (`Encrypted`) returned by the `describe_file_systems` call. The audit steps explicitly mention CLI-based verification, and AWS Config provides a managed rule for this control. There is no need for manual inspection, as all necessary information is programmatically retrievable.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
aws_efs_file_system_encryption_at_rest_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **AWS Resource/Service:** Elastic File System (EFS) file systems
- **API/CLI Method:** 
  - boto3: `efs.describe_file_systems()`
  - AWS CLI: `aws efs describe-file-systems --region <region>`
- **Properties to Check:**  
  - Each file system's `Encrypted` property in the response.
- **Pass Criteria:**  
  - All EFS file systems in all regions have `"Encrypted": true`.
- **Fail Criteria:**  
  - Any EFS file system in any region has `"Encrypted": false`.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
There were no disagreements between the two AI opinions; both supported automation and provided technically accurate reasoning.

**SOURCE:** CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
